name: roadmap_query
description: READ-ONLY. The PRECISE tool for Roadmap-card questions — always prefer this over notion_search for anything about cards on the Roadmap board: card status ('what's WIP / under review'), a specific card's Design Status, who's on a card, finding a card from a vague or partial title, or a card number. Queries the live Roadmap database directly and returns a COMPLETE result set (not a keyword sample): title, link, card number, Design Status, Dev Status, pillars, and people per card. Title lookups return ranked fuzzy candidates — when a user describes a card vaguely, use these to suggest the closest matches with links ('did you mean…') instead of asking for a link. Combine filters freely (e.g. design_status + person). Safe to call freely (read-only, no gate); 1-2 subrequests total, far cheaper than repeated searches.

design_status.description: Filter by exact Design Status column value, e.g. 'WIP', 'Under Review', 'Ready for Design', 'Under Dev', 'Shipped'. If the value is wrong, the error lists the valid options — retry with one.

title.description: Full, partial, or vague card title/description — fuzzy-matched against every card on the board; returns ranked candidates.

person.description: A person's name (or part of it) — matches cards where they appear in any people column (Contributor, Dev, …).

card_number.description: The card's numeric id as shown on the board (e.g. 2436).

name: notion_search
description: READ-ONLY. Search Notion for pages and database entries by keyword when you do NOT already have a URL. Prefer a narrow `scope` when you know the surface — scoped queries hit the live database directly (complete scan + fuzzy title match) and beat workspace search. Scopes: 'team' (SME roster), 'apps' (access-request routing — Application Admin grants; you only route), 'marketplace' (prototype catalog), 'help_tutors' / 'help_teachers' (Help Center articles), 'decisions' (Decisions DB), 'running_notes' (Design Running Notes), 'news' / 'success_stories' / 'research_papers' / 'banners' (Content HQ read radar), 'any' (workspace title search — last resort). NOT for Roadmap cards — use roadmap_query. Returns titles + URLs (+ key properties); follow up with source_read for full content. Only pages the uno-bot integration is connected to are searchable; if nothing comes back, say so rather than guessing. Safe to call freely (read-only, no gate).

query.description: What to find, in plain words, e.g. 'PRD for tutor matching', 'onboarding decision', 'high-fi toolkit prototypes', 'session count help article'.

scope.description: 'team' = Team Member roster. 'apps' = Third Party Applications (access routing). 'marketplace' = Prototype Marketplace. 'help_tutors' / 'help_teachers' = Help Center article DBs. 'decisions' = Decisions DB. 'running_notes' = Design Running Notes. 'news' / 'success_stories' / 'research_papers' / 'banners' = Content HQ. 'any' = workspace-wide title search (prefer a catalog scope when you know the surface). Card lookups → roadmap_query.

name: source_read
description: READ-ONLY. Fetch the actual CONTENT of a link before answering — a Notion page (returns title + properties incl. Owner/people + block text), a Figma frame (returns node name/type + text layers), a GitHub/raw file, or any web URL (returns text). Use this WHENEVER the request contains or references a URL, a linked PRD/doc/guide, or a Figma frame: read it and ground your answer in the returned content, then cite it. Never answer a question about a linked source from memory/priors. For 'who owns/should review this' questions, read the page and use its Owner/people property rather than guessing. Safe to call freely (read-only, no gate).

url.description: The full http(s) URL to read (Notion, Figma, GitHub, or any web page).

name: blueprint_search
description: READ-ONLY. Query the uno-blueprint (the Plus product/design source of truth in Supabase) for grounded facts about product state, features, decisions, and status. Use this to answer or justify factual and 'where are we on X' questions from the blueprint FIRST, before relying on loaded docs, and ALWAYS cite the row(s) you used. If it returns no rows or reports it isn't configured, say so and fall back to cited docs or 'I don't know' — never fabricate. Safe to call freely (read-only, no gate).

query.description: Free-text query describing what you need from the blueprint, e.g. 'Tutor Admin redesign status', 'decisions about empty states'.

name: github_read
description: READ-ONLY. Read from the plus-uno GitHub repo — fetch a file's contents by repo-relative path (DS cheat-sheets, rule docs, AGENTS.md, a component's source), or list a directory to check what EXISTS (a directory path returns its entries automatically). Use it to confirm a design-system component before proposing component_implement (read `design-system/src/components`), or to ground a review in the actual rule docs rather than memory. For an arbitrary GitHub URL a designer pasted, prefer source_read; use github_read when you know the path or need a directory listing. Safe to call freely (read-only, no gate).

ref.description: Optional git ref (branch/tag/sha). Defaults to the repo's default branch.

search.description: Code-search mode: find WHERE something lives in the repo (e.g. 'focus-ring', 'PageLayout') — returns matching file paths + github.com links to read or cite. When set, 'path' is ignored.

name: slack_user_profile
description: READ-ONLY. Look up a Slack user's profile by id — display name, title/role, email, timezone, status. Use when you need to know WHO someone is (a member id from slack_channel_members, an @-mention in the thread, a card contributor's Slack identity). Mention people as <@id> in replies. Safe to call freely (read-only, no gate).

user_id.description: Slack user id (U…), bare or as an <@U…> mention.

name: slack_channel_members
description: READ-ONLY. List a Slack channel's member ids (first 100) — for 'who's in #plus-design', review-routing, or checking whether someone is in a channel before referring them there. The bot must be in the channel. Returns ids; mention them as <@id> (Slack renders names), and use slack_user_profile for a specific person's role — don't fetch every profile. Safe to call freely (read-only, no gate).

channel_id.description: Slack channel id (C…), bare or as a <#C…> mention.

name: slack_thread_read
description: READ-ONLY. Read the messages of a Slack thread you were given a link to (outside this conversation's own memory) — to tally handoff sign-offs (dev/PM/stakeholder ✅), read reviewer verdicts, or pull a source discussion to synthesize into findings. Pass the Slack message/thread permalink. The bot must be a member of the channel. Safe to call freely (read-only, no gate).

link.description: A Slack message or thread permalink, e.g. 'https://plus-team.slack.com/archives/C03FC8AS69K/p1720000000000000'.

name: slack_react
description: Add an emoji reaction AS UNO-BOT to a message in the current thread. Use freely to acknowledge, celebrate, or signal state — any workspace emoji name (without colons), e.g. 'tada', 'eyes', 'raised_hands', or a custom workspace emoji. Reactions are reversible and ungated. Do NOT react with white_check_mark or x on proposal cards (those are reserved for confirm/cancel). If message_ts is omitted, reacts to the user's current message.

emoji.description: Emoji name without colons, e.g. 'tada' or 'rocket'.

message_ts.description: Optional Slack ts of the message to react to (must be in the current channel). Defaults to the user's current message.

name: slack_search
description: Search Slack workspace messages. Results are PRE-CLEARED for sharing: public channels plus the team-designated private channels only — DMs and other private spaces are filtered out before you see them, so everything returned is safe to quote in your reply. The response includes withheld_private_matches (a count of hits that existed in non-shareable spaces); if it's >0 and relevant, you may say matches exist in private spaces you can't surface — never speculate about their content. Use for 'find the discussion about X', sign-off tallies across channels, locating past decisions. READ-ONLY, ungated.

query.description: Slack search query. Supports operators like in:#channel, from:@user, before:/after: dates.

name: notion_create
description: Create a new page or card — SIDE EFFECT, routed through the confirmation gate. `surface` selects WHERE and WHAT is created: 'prd' (design PRD on the Roadmap board), 'intake' (maintenance intake on the Roadmap board), or 'decision' (Decisions DB row under Design HQ — requires properties.roadmap_card). Research docs and marketplace entries are NOT bot surfaces — they run in-IDE. Draft the content conversationally first and let the designer refine it; only invoke once they approve. Follow docs/conventions/notion.md for required properties and body shape — NEVER create select options / pillars / OKRs; exact-match existing option names. Keep content grounded in the conversation; put genuine unknowns under an 'Open Questions' section rather than inventing.

surface.description: Where to file it: 'prd' (feature or DS-component PRD card on the Roadmap board), 'intake' (maintenance/request intake card, Product Pillar: Universal + Product Tag: Maintenance), or 'decision' (Decisions DB row — requires properties.roadmap_card; optional Status/Evidence). Research docs and marketplace entries are created in-IDE, not by the bot.

summary.description: 1-3 sentence overview, rendered as the opening of the body.

sections.description: Body sections in order (Problem/Context, Goals, Users & Scenarios, Requirements, Open Questions, etc.). Each body may contain multiple paragraphs (separate with blank lines).

acceptance_criteria.description: For 'prd'/'prd' (component PRDs included): checklist rendered as Notion to-do checkboxes (read downstream by component_implement / prototype_scaffold).

properties.description: Surface-specific card properties as key→value, exact-matched against the live schema by the tool (PRD: product_pillar; intake: evidence_link, suggested_tier; decision: roadmap_card [required], status, evidence). Only include what the conversation supports; never invent option names.

source_url.description: Optional link back to the source — a Slack permalink to the discussion, or a Figma link.

name: notion_update
description: Update an existing Notion page/card — SIDE EFFECT, routed through the confirmation gate. Works on ANY page or database the bot is shared on (not just the Roadmap board): Roadmap cards, Decisions DB rows, per-person running-notes pages, any DB. Two uses, combinable: (1) change PROPERTIES via `properties` — the tool reads the page's live database schema and matches your property name to it case/space/underscore-insensitively, then writes by the REAL type, so "Dev Status", "Dev_Status", a Date, a Person, a URL, a number, a checkbox all work. It NEVER invents a select/status option — if a value isn't an existing option it reports back instead of guessing (Notion auto-creates on mismatch). (2) append narrative blocks via `append` — append, never overwrite a human-owned body. Pass the page URL.

page_url.description: The Notion URL (or page id) of the card/page to update.

properties.description: Property changes as name→value (string values). The tool matches each name to the page's live database schema (case/space/underscore-insensitive) and formats by the real Notion type — status, select, multi_select (comma-separated), date (ISO, or "start → end"), number, checkbox, url, email, rich text, title. select/status/multi_select values must be EXISTING options (unknown ones are reported, never created). People/relation need a Notion id or URL.

append.description: Narrative blocks to append to the end of the page body.

text.description: A single block of text to append (use when no heading is needed, e.g. a dated progress pulse).

name: notion_archive
description: Archive (soft-delete) a Notion page/card — SIDE EFFECT, routed through the confirmation gate. Use to undo or remove a page the designer asks to delete ('delete that', 'remove that card', 'undo'). Works on any page the bot can reach, not just cards it created. Archiving moves the page to Notion's trash (recoverable); the ✅ confirmation gate + requester identity are the safety. Pass the page URL (typically the link the bot posted when it created the card).

page_url.description: The Notion URL (or page id) of the card to archive.

name: component_implement
description: Open a draft PR that implements a named design-system component change, by triggering the figma-implement GitHub Action — SIDE EFFECT, routed through the confirmation gate. Use ONLY when the designer clearly asks for a specific named component (e.g. 'implement Badge', 'go ahead with the Badge change'). REQUIRES a Notion PRD: the polling bot creates one and posts it in #uno-bot. If the thread already has that PRD notification, proceed (the Worker reads it from the thread); if there is NO PRD, do NOT invoke — ask for the PRD link first, then pass notion_prd_url. Confirm the component exists first with github_read (list design-system/src/components) if unsure. Never invent the component name or PRD URL. For a NEW prototype from a Figma frame, use prototype_scaffold instead.

component.description: The component name as it appears in the Figma library, exact casing (e.g. 'Badge', 'Button', 'CardSurface').

notion_prd_url.description: The Notion PRD link for this change. Usually read automatically from the polling-bot notification in the thread — only set this when the designer pastes a PRD link themselves.

notes.description: Optional free-form implementation notes from the designer. Pass through verbatim if present; omit otherwise.

name: prototype_scaffold
description: Scaffold a NEW prototypes prototype from a Figma design (page/screen/flow), by triggering the figma-implement-design GitHub Action to open a draft PR — SIDE EFFECT, routed through the confirmation gate. Use when the designer pastes a Figma URL and asks for a prototype (e.g. 'scaffold a prototypes for this frame', 'build this design <figma.com/...>'). A Notion PRD link is OPTIONAL but preferred — ask if they have one for better context; if not, offer to build straight from the frame and, on their OK, invoke with notion_prd_url omitted. Never invent a PRD URL. Do NOT use for named DS-library component work — that is component_implement.

figma_url.description: Full Figma URL the designer pasted, e.g. 'https://www.figma.com/design/NUcRDZUzUiuVmx1ydNXpl3/APPLICATION-PROTOTYPES?node-id=158-21725'. Must contain a node-id query param.

notion_prd_url.description: Optional. Full notion.so URL of a PRD for this prototype, if the designer has one. Never invent it; omit if they choose to skip.

slug.description: Optional kebab-case folder name for prototypes/{slug}/. Must match ^[a-z0-9][a-z0-9-]{1,40}$. If omitted, derived from the Figma node name.

notes.description: Optional free-form implementation notes from the designer. Pass through verbatim.

name: shareout_post
description: Share a prototype/design/PRD with the team FOR FEEDBACK (the 'publish' / 'share out' / 'post for feedback' action). Posts a shareout to #plus-design-feedback: what it is, a link back, a feedback prompt, and @-mentioned reviewers — SIDE EFFECT (it pings people), routed through the confirmation gate. This is NOT marketplace registration: use this when the designer wants eyes/critique/feedback — catalog registration runs in-IDE via writers/notion, not through any bot tool. Draft the shareout text conversationally first; invoke once they approve.

summary.description: One or two sentences: what is being shared and what feedback is wanted.

link.description: Link to the artifact (prototype URL, Figma frame, PR, or PRD).

reviewers.description: Reviewers to tag — Slack user IDs (U…) ONLY, never display names (a plain name renders as dead text and pings nobody). Resolve IDs via notion_search(scope:'team') slackUserId or the PRD Owner before invoking; if you can't resolve an ID, leave the person out and say so in the preview.

deadline.description: Optional human-readable feedback deadline, e.g. 'EOD Friday'.

name: email_send
description: Send an email via Gmail on the design team's behalf — SIDE EFFECT (sends a real email), routed through the confirmation gate. Use for outward notifications a Slack message can't cover: emailing an external SME/collaborator, sending a PRD or prototype summary to a stakeholder, or a follow-up the designer explicitly asks to send by email. Draft the email conversationally first (recipient, subject, body) and let the designer refine it; only invoke once they approve. Never invent recipient addresses — ask for them if unknown. Prefer Slack for anything that can stay in Slack.

to.description: Recipient email address(es). Never invent — use addresses the designer provided.

body.description: Plain-text email body. Write it out fully — no placeholders.

name: proposal_resolve
description: Resolve a side-effect proposal that is awaiting user confirmation. Use ONLY when the system prompt indicates a pending proposal AND the user's current message clearly expresses a decision about it ('go ahead', 'yes do it', 'ship it', 'no, cancel', 'nevermind'). Anyone in the thread may confirm or cancel — not just the original requester. If the user asks an unrelated question or is still discussing without deciding, do NOT invoke — reply conversationally and the proposal sits until ✅, ❌, or 60-minute expiry.

decision.description: 'confirm' to proceed with the proposed action as-is. 'cancel' to stop or change direction (for a parameter change, cancel then stage a fresh proposal).

message_to_user.description: Short Slack mrkdwn message to post alongside the resolution, e.g. 'Got it — firing the implement for Badge now.' Keep to one or two sentences.
